# The frequentist RVE lineage owns it {#sec-rve-lineage}

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Guardrails honored in this chapter

- **G-15** — RVE is asymptotically consistent in *m* (the number of studies), not
  *k* (the number of effect sizes); in small-*m* meta-analysis (especially *m* < 8)
  Type I error inflates unless a **named** small-sample correction (HTZ, AHZ, CWB, or
  CR2) is applied. This chapter never asserts that uncorrected RVE controls Type I
  error in the small-*m* regime; every adequacy claim names its correction in the same
  sentence — the spine guard of the chapter.
- **G-03** — Pustejovsky and Chen (2024, Theorem 2) proved the aggregated and
  correlated-effects likelihoods *proportional* — equal up to a parameter-free constant
  κ — never identical; exact identity is reserved downstream for this review's own
  unit-weight-boundary result and is never read into the field's proportionality result.
- **G-04 / G-14** — CR2 (Bell–McCaffrey) is a *design-specific* comparator: it improves
  coverage on balanced designs but degrades under severe imbalance or skewed regressors,
  and its equality conditions hold only under compound-symmetric within-subgroup blocks
  and constant subgroup size; it is never universal, never covers all designs, and is
  never the calibration target.

This chapter also carries forward **G-05** (when moderators vary within study it is the
*aggregated* representation that ceases to exist, never the correlated-effects model)
and **G-25** (the Godambe / estimating-equation substrate *motivates* the need for a
correction; it does not supply one).
:::

This chapter establishes the first leg of the funnel: the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich
covariance that any Bayesian analogue must match is a *settled, owned* frequentist
standard. It is owned not by a single paper but by a lineage: one apparatus that
matured, link by link, from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson's origin through Tipton's
small-sample discipline and Pustejovsky and Tipton's working-model consolidation to
Pustejovsky and Chen's integrative capstone. The chronology carries the argument. Each link *extends rather than displaces* the one before it, so that what
arrives at the end is the completion of a single standard rather than a competing estimator.
The object that lineage owns is a specific covariance estimand, the marginal RVE/CHE
sandwich covariance of the effect-size vector, and the conditions under which each of
its properties holds are stated with care below, because it is that estimand,
with those conditions, that any Bayesian analogue must measure itself against.
The seven sections trace that maturation: what the lineage made the field-default, what
its sandwich targets, why the target is robust, how it disciplined the small-sample
regime, why CR2 is a comparator and not the target, which working model survives where
the other breaks, and the degrees-of-freedom tuning and canonical software that
instantiate the whole apparatus. This chapter renders the standard; it does not re-prove
it.

## 2.1 The established field-default {#sec-field-default}

Robust variance estimation is the established frequentist field-default for
meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes, built from the cluster-sandwich and refined
across the lineage from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson [-@hedges2010] through Tipton
[-@tipton2015a; -@tipton2015b], Pustejovsky and Tipton [-@pustejovsky2022], to
Pustejovsky and Chen [-@pustejovsky2024]; its field-default status is documented both in
the methodological lineage and in the practitioner-tutorial literature
[@tannersmith2016] [([CL-P1-01](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-01), POSITIONING)]{.claim-tag}. This is a framing claim about the structure
of the frequentist literature, not a program theorem: the status is carried as
POSITIONING, and the origin paper's own theorem is held at the field level, never
re-elevated.

@hedges2010 is the origin: it establishes the
cluster-sandwich as the frequentist apparatus for dependent-effect meta-regression,
furnishing both a correlated/observation-level working model and the cluster-level
residual-cross-product sandwich that estimates the marginal covariance of the
effect-size vector. Tipton then disciplined the small-sample regime that the origin
result left asymptotic [@tipton2015a] and extended the standard to multiple-contrast
tests [@tipton2015b], each move *extending* rather than overturning Hedges, Tipton and
Johnson. Pustejovsky and Tipton [-@pustejovsky2022] consolidated the working-model
choices — correlated-effects, hierarchical-effects, and their combination — into a single
deployable framework, and Pustejovsky and Chen [-@pustejovsky2024] integrated the
apparatus into one treatment of aggregated and correlated-effects representations,
supplying the analytic equivalence and design-class results this chapter leans on. The
status is documented from two directions at once. The methodological lineage names and
extends the chain: the more recent multivariate and cross-domain entries
[@chen2025; @welz2023; @yang2024] inherit the same sandwich without overturning it, and
Pustejovsky [-@pustejovsky2025] names the same lineage as the "well-developed and readily
available" robustness strand for dependent effects. From the other direction, the
practitioner tutorials [@tannersmith2014; @tannersmith2016] and applied syntheses
[@gnambs2023; @cameron2015] document cross-disciplinary adoption across education,
psychology, health, and the microeconometric clustering literature from which the
cluster-sandwich also descends, and the canonical software lineage
[@joshi2022; @fernandezcastilla2026; @williams2025a] carries it into routine use.

The field-default claim is made honestly, with its adoption qualifier intact and stated
as a precondition rather than a concession. Tipton, Pustejovsky and Ahmadi [-@tipton2019] record
that RVE was endorsed in principle among the consensus-recommended modeling alternatives
for dependent effects, yet appeared in only about five percent of syntheses as of 2016.
"Field-default" therefore denotes methodological consensus and emerging adoption (the
standard the field recognizes as correct for dependent effects, the one the methods
literature converged on and the tutorials teach) rather than universal practitioner
uptake. The qualifier sharpens the lineage claim rather than weakening it. The review
measures a Bayesian analogue against the *estimand* this lineage fixed, an object whose
authority rests on the lineage's structural coherence, not on a head-count of applied
syntheses.

## 2.2 What the sandwich targets {#sec-what-targets}

The lineage having fixed *which* apparatus is standard, the next link fixes *what* that
apparatus targets. The RVE sandwich targets the marginal covariance of the effect-size
vector through a working model — aggregated or correlated-effects — that may misspecify
the within-study correlation, and is asymptotically consistent in *m*, the number of
studies, without requiring the true within-study correlation to be known [([CL-P1-02](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-02),
CONDITIONAL; G-03, G-15)]{.claim-tag}. Pustejovsky and Chen [-@pustejovsky2024] state the property
directly: the sandwich does not rely on the assumed dependence structure being correctly
specified, and it requires a large number of independent clusters. The covariance the
sandwich estimates is the *marginal* covariance — the object that survives integrating
over whatever within-study correlation the working model posits — and naming it as
marginal is a standing discipline of this book. Two preconditions on that consistency are
load-bearing and must be kept distinct.

The consistency runs in *m* (studies), not in *k* (effect sizes). The estimand is
a marginal covariance estimated from cluster-level residual cross-products, and the origin
result is explicitly contingent on regularity conditions that bite only as the number of
independent studies grows [@hedges2010; @pustejovsky2022]. A synthesis with twelve studies
reporting ten effects each has *k* = 120 rows in its data frame but *m* = 12 independent
clusters; it is the twelve that the asymptotics count. The cross-domain analogues confirm
the same direction under the clusters↔studies mapping: econometric clustering theory makes
consistency contingent on the number of clusters diverging, not the number of observations
[@cameron2015], and the survey design-based sandwich is consistent in the survey
central-limit sense, in the number of sampled units [@binder1983], with clusters and strata
playing the role of studies, never effect sizes. Eliding *m* into *k* would misstate the
asymptotic regime, and it is precisely the regime where the small-*m* discipline of §2.4
becomes binding (G-15).

What Pustejovsky and Chen establish about the two working-model
likelihoods is a proportionality, not an identity. Their Theorem 2 shows the aggregated
and correlated-effects likelihoods are *proportional — equal up to a parameter-free
constant κ*, and not identical (G-03). The distinction is load-bearing for the
entire book: proportionality up to a constant suffices for the maximizing arguments and
the sandwich target to agree, which is what licenses moving between the two
representations, but it is strictly weaker than a likelihood identity. Exact identity is a
separate, stronger statement reserved downstream for this review's own unit-weight-boundary
result; it must never be read back into the field's proportionality theorem. The
cross-domain analogues confirm the weaker, correct reading: econometric clustering
[@cameron2015] and survey design-based estimation [@binder1983] each affirm consistency in
the number of clusters, and neither asserts a likelihood identity. What the sandwich
targets, then, is a single marginal covariance, reached through either of two
proportionally related working models, consistent as studies accumulate.

## 2.3 Why the sandwich is robust {#sec-why-robust}

Why the marginal sandwich is robust is the link that explains why the lineage's target is
worth calibrating to at all. The sandwich estimator remains consistent under essentially
arbitrary misspecification of the within-study correlation structure (which is almost
never known in meta-analytic practice), provided the working model's marginal moments are
calibrated to the data-generating process [@hong2018; @cameron2015; @pustejovsky2022]
[([CL-P1-03](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-03), CONDITIONAL; G-25)]{.claim-tag}. This is the sandwich's central virtue: its validity does
not turn on getting the dependence structure right, only on the marginal moments being
approximately correct. @hong2018 states the rationale plainly: the estimator is
asymptotically correct even when the working likelihood is incorrect, and the
consolidating lineage [@pustejovsky2022] carries it as an asymptotic-in-*m* property of the
field. The robustness is asymptotic and conditional on calibrated marginal moments; it is
not unconditional, and the prose states the condition with the claim.

The deeper rationale is supplied by the estimating-equation and Godambe-information
machinery, of which the design-based sandwich of @binder1983 is the survey-side origin.
That substrate gives the asymptotic justification for why covariance recalibration under
working-model misspecification is *principled* rather than ad hoc: when an estimator solves
an estimating equation whose score is correct in expectation, its sampling covariance takes
the Godambe sandwich form regardless of whether the full working likelihood is correct,
given correctly specified low-order margins. This is the precise sense in which the
substrate *motivates the need* for a correction — it establishes that a marginal covariance
target is well-defined and that realigning toward it is licensed by estimating-equation
theory — without itself *supplying* a finite-sample correction (G-25). The literature
carries it as motivation, never as proof that a finite-sample correction already exists
[@joshi2022]. This misspecification-robustness, asymptotic, conditional, and principled,
is what makes the marginal sandwich covariance an attractive calibration target for
a Bayesian analogue: it is the covariance an estimating-equation argument says inference
*should* report under misspecification. It is cited here as why-recalibration-is-needed
framing, the hinge the calibrated-Bayes pillar (P3) later inherits, not as evidence
the correction has already been built.

## 2.4 The small-*m* discipline {#sec-small-m}

The robustness of §2.3 is asymptotic, and the asymptotics bite slowly, which is why the
lineage's most consequential refinement was the small-sample discipline. In small-*m*
meta-analysis, especially *m* below eight, RVE inflates Type I error unless a named
small-sample correction is applied: HTZ, AHZ, CWB, or CR2 (Bell–McCaffrey; @imbens2016);
uncorrected RVE does not control Type I error when studies are few [([CL-P1-04](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-04), EMPIRICAL;
G-15)]{.claim-tag}. This is the chapter's spine guard, and it is stated qualitatively by discipline:
uncorrected small-*m* RVE *over-rejects*, and level control *recovers only after a named
correction*. No bare coverage percentage is asserted, and no sentence claims small-*m*
adequacy without naming the specific adjustment that delivers it.

@tipton2015a is the primary named source. Tipton showed by simulation that uncorrected
robust inference over-rejects badly in small-*m* meta-regression and that level control
recovers only via the bias-reduced residual corrections together with Satterthwaite degrees
of freedom — the named HTZ-type adjustment. Each subsequent link in the lineage named its
own correction, again *extending* the discipline rather than replacing it. @tipton2015b
extended it to multiple-contrast tests with the AHZ correction, showing uncorrected
multiple-contrast RVE liberal even at large *m*. @joshi2022 carried the cluster wild
bootstrap (CWB) as a named small-*m* alternative, directly stating that small-*m* RVE
inflates Type I error and is controlled only through a named correction. @yang2024 confirmed
that the bare cluster-robust estimator is downwardly biased and fails Type I control unless
the number of independent studies is large or a named correction is applied. Beneath this
meta-analytic lineage sits its methodological origin: @bell2002 derived the bias-reduced
linearization later named CR2 and showed by simulation that uncorrected small-sample
linearization inflates Type I error, and @pustejovsky2018 implemented CR2 with
Satterthwaite/AHT degrees of freedom and documented the over-rejection of the uncorrected
Wald test.

The same discipline is corroborated, in its own idiom, by adjacent settings, and the
boundary between the four endorsed corrections and these analogues is held explicit. The
econometric clustering literature from which the cluster-sandwich descends finds that
few-cluster Wald tests over-reject unless a named correction — the cluster jackknife CV3
with a *t*(G−1) reference [@mackinnon2023a; @mackinnon2025] or the Bell–McCaffrey
degrees-of-freedom adjustment [@cameron2015] — is applied; small-*k* scalar-summary intervals
undercover unless an adjusted reference distribution is used [@zejnullahi2024]; the
general-meta-analytic precursor literature on liberal small-sample tests names its own
remedy, the Hartung *q*-variance estimator with a *t* reference [@hartung2001]; and the
multilevel literature warns that ignoring the within-study sampling covariance underestimates
standard errors and inflates Type I error [@vandennoortgate2015]. These cross-domain
corrections are corroboration that the small-sample failure and its named remedies are
general; the CV3 jackknife and the *t*(G−1) reference in particular are kept clearly labeled
as econometric analogues, not as one of the four meta-analytic corrections the review endorses
under G-15. What a working meta-analyst must take from this section is a rule, not a number:
in the small-*m* regime, RVE adequacy is a claim about RVE-plus-a-named-correction, never
about bare RVE. An adequacy claim that does not name its correction is not a claim the test supports.

## 2.5 CR2 is a comparator, not the target {#sec-cr2-comparator}

Among the named corrections, CR2 warrants particular care, because it is the comparator most
easily mistaken for a universal fix. The CR2 correction (Bell–McCaffrey; @imbens2016) improves
coverage on balanced designs but degrades under severe imbalance or skewed regressors, and its
compound-symmetric and constant-subgroup-size conditions are not always met [([CL-P1-05](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-05),
CONDITIONAL; G-04, G-14)]{.claim-tag}. Even at its canonical development the correction is not universal:
@imbens2016 show that Bell–McCaffrey is nearly inert on balanced designs, helps most under skew
or imbalance, yet itself undercovers under skewed *errors* at one extreme and is conservative at
the other. The underlying bias-reduced linearization is *exactly* unbiased only under independent
and identically distributed errors [@bell2002], so the equality it promises is a design-specific
property, not a guarantee that holds across designs.

The conditions under which CR2 achieves its equivalence are pinned by the lineage's capstone.
Pustejovsky and Chen [-@pustejovsky2024, Theorem 3] establish that the CR2-type equivalence holds
*only* under compound-symmetric within-subgroup blocks *and* constant subgroup size — conditions
the paper characterizes as more restrictive and artificial, and which fail in Pustejovsky and
Chen's own worked examples (G-04). Where those conditions break, so does the equality. The
degradation is documented across designs: coverage falls materially below nominal in small-*k*
multivariate meta-regression [@welz2023], and most starkly in crossed phylogenetic designs, where
CR2 is inapplicable and cluster-robust inference falls below nominal regardless [@williams2025a].
Because CR2's accuracy is contingent on design balance, it cannot serve as ground truth; the prose
never describes it as universal, as covering all designs, or as eliminating Type I error (G-14).
CR2 is therefore a design-specific tuning and an external comparator — useful where its design
conditions hold, degraded where they do not — never a universal fix and never the calibration
target. This is the distinction the chapter carries forward: the object a Bayesian analogue
must match is the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance *itself*, not CR2 treated as ground truth.
CR2 is one design-aware way the frequentist lineage approximates that covariance in finite samples;
it is not the covariance.

## 2.6 The design-class distinction {#sec-design-class}

The lineage also fixes *which working model survives* when the data structure is most demanding,
a distinction this review later inherits as the warrant for observation-level Bayesian structure.
Dependent effects are handled by either the aggregated (one-row-per-study) or the correlated-effects
(observation-level) working model; when moderators vary within study, the aggregated representation
ceases to exist, whereas the correlated-effects model remains well-defined with the sandwich applied
at the observation level [@pustejovsky2024] [([CL-P1-06](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-06), CONDITIONAL; G-05)]{.claim-tag}. The mechanism is plain: the aggregated one-row-per-study representation requires
study-level predictors, so when a moderator takes different values across the effect sizes *within*
the same study, there is no single study-level row that can represent it, and the aggregated
representation ceases to correspond to any model. The correlated-effects model, working at the level
of the individual effect size, retains the full within-study design matrix and simply keeps the rows.

The direction of this result is locked (G-05): it is *aggregation* that breaks,
and the correlated-effects model that survives, never the reverse. Inverting it (claiming the
correlated-effects model fails) would misstate the entire design-class result and quietly undercut
the observation-level structure this review carries into its Bayesian analogue. The "ceases to exist"
result itself is sourced uniquely to Pustejovsky and Chen [-@pustejovsky2024], the capstone of the
lineage; the earlier links and the parallel literatures furnish the two representations and
corroborate the direction without asserting the analytic result themselves. @hedges2010 supplies both
the correlated/observation-level working model and the synthetic aggregated alternative but does not
state the direction, which is Pustejovsky and Chen's contribution; and the multilevel route reaches
the same design-class distinction from the hierarchical side, with @vandennoortgate2015 showing that
aggregating effect sizes loses information and can make moderator estimation impossible while the
observation-level model survives, the same direction, never inverted. For a practitioner with
within-study moderator variation, the operational consequence is direct: the observation-level
correlated-effects model is not merely the more flexible choice but the *only* well-defined one.
This survival of observation-level structure is the analytic warrant for the Bayesian observation-level
model this review carries into P4: a Bayesian analogue that operates at the observation level is
calibrating to a target that remains well-defined precisely where the aggregated alternative cannot.

## 2.7 Satterthwaite df tuning and canonical tooling {#sec-df-tooling}

Operating the sandwich in finite samples requires a reference distribution, and the lineage's routine
device for setting it is the last refinement to trace, together with the software that instantiates
the whole apparatus. The Satterthwaite degrees-of-freedom approximation is routinely applied in
frequentist RVE meta-regression to set the effective degrees of freedom for tests and confidence
intervals; it is asymptotically justified but biased under severe imbalance or small *m*, becoming
unreliable once the effective degrees of freedom fall below roughly four [@tipton2015a; @pustejovsky2018]
[([CL-P1-07](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-07), CONDITIONAL; G-15)]{.claim-tag}. Here too the chronology is one of refinement: the origin paper
[@hedges2010] supplied only the crude *m* − *p* correction; the coefficient-by-coefficient Satterthwaite
approximation, and the finding that the effective df are covariate-dependent and often far below *m* − 1,
are established by @tipton2015a and @pustejovsky2018, with @joshi2022 corroborating both the routine use
and the small-*m* below-nominal behavior. The practical rule that falls out, familiar from any
`clubSandwich` or `robumeta` warning, is to distrust a test whose effective df drop below about
four, because the Satterthwaite reference is no longer reliable there. This df-below-four fragility is
exactly the kind of finite-sample imperfection in the field-default tooling whose own limits under small
*m* and imbalance are part of the standing small-sample discipline (G-15), and it ties directly back to
§2.4.

That entire apparatus — sandwich, named corrections, Satterthwaite df — is, finally, instantiated in
canonical software, which is what carries the standard from the methods literature into a practitioner's
analysis [([CL-P1-08](98-appendix-claim-index.qmd#cl-p1-08), POSITIONING)]{.claim-tag}. The `clubSandwich` package (Pustejovsky and Tipton) and `robumeta`
(Fisher and Tipton) are the canonical implementations of frequentist RVE with small-sample corrections
(CR2, HTZ/AHZ, and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom) and the field-standard tooling for dependent-effect
meta-analysis [@pustejovsky2018; @joshi2022; @tannersmith2016; @welz2023]. Pustejovsky and Tipton
[-@pustejovsky2018] are the methods source that introduces and implements CR2 with AHT degrees of freedom
in `clubSandwich`; Joshi and colleagues [-@joshi2022] and Tanner-Smith and Tipton [-@tannersmith2016] name
and use both `clubSandwich` and `robumeta` as the standard implementations actually deployed; @welz2023
names `clubSandwich` as the canonical software for cluster-robust estimation with small-sample
corrections; and the field-tooling record [@tipton2019; @fernandezcastilla2026], with Pustejovsky and
Tipton [-@pustejovsky2022], situates them in the broader practitioner ecosystem. The lineage thus closes
the loop a practitioner cares about: a fixed estimand, a disciplined small-sample regime with named
corrections, a monitored reference distribution, and shipped software that does all three from a single
deployable toolchain.

Taken together, CL-P1-01 through CL-P1-08 establish a complete, instantiated, software-backed standard,
and they fix a single object: a marginal cluster-sandwich, consistent in *m* under working-model
misspecification, robust under calibrated marginal moments, proportional-not-identical across its two
working-model representations, requiring a named small-sample correction at low *m*, with CR2 as a
design-specific comparator rather than the target, the aggregated representation that breaks where the
correlated-effects model survives, the Satterthwaite tuning with its df-below-four fragility, and
`clubSandwich` and `robumeta` as canonical tooling. This is the owned frequentist standard — one apparatus
matured into a field-default through the lineage from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson to Pustejovsky and Chen.
The marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance it targets is precisely the covariance target a Bayesian analogue
would have to match, which is the question P2 takes up: across a broad survey of Bayesian meta-analysis,
is there any published method that adopts this sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target?
